_meta.swap_instructions: Update GOLD_ANCHOR in rubrics.py when real top-performing ads arrive from Slack.
_meta.data_integrity_note: ONLY use metrics listed in approved_differentiators.metrics. Do NOT invent statistics, guarantees, or discounts.

brand.name: Varsity Tutors
brand.platform: Facebook and Instagram paid social ads

tone_descriptors: Empowering — we believe every student can improve
Knowledgeable — we speak with expertise, not arrogance
Approachable — we are warm and human, never corporate
Results-focused — we lead with outcomes, never features
writing_principles: Lead with outcomes, not features — say what the student gains, not what we offer
Use specific numbers over vague adjectives — '200+ points' not 'better scores'
Speak to the parent's anxiety AND the student's ambition — both are in the room
Confident but not arrogant — expert but not elitist
One clear message per ad — never compete with yourself

Synthesize product details into natural copy — the product field is a fact sheet, not a script. Extract ONE primary benefit most relevant to this audience. Translate metrics into the audience's language.
Numbers survive verbatim — '200+ points', '3.4M ratings', '24 hours', '5%'. Everything else gets rewritten.
Write TO the audience, never ABOUT them — the audience field tells you who you are writing for, not content to reference directly in the ad.

metrics: Top 5% tutors — rigorously vetted, not an open marketplace
Matched with a tutor in 24 hours based on exact weak areas
1-on-1 personalized matching — not a one-size-fits-all course
prohibited_claims: Do NOT invent statistics not listed above
Do NOT promise score guarantees or refunds
Do NOT use percentage claims not listed above (e.g. '90% of students...')
Do NOT fabricate discounts or limited-time offers
Do NOT mention competitor names in ad copy

hook_guidelines.general_rule: The hook must appear and be complete within the first 100 characters of primary_text. This is the Meta truncation threshold — everything after is hidden behind 'See More'.

allowed: Questions that surface a real anxiety the parent or student is already feeling
Questions about outcomes: score, college, readiness
Questions that position Varsity Tutors as the obvious answer
forbidden: Questions that feel accusatory or make the parent feel guilty
Rhetorical questions with no clear answer path
Any statistic not in the approved list

allowed: Third-person student transformation stories (no real names — fictional composite)
Parent perspective stories about their child's improvement
Score improvement arcs: starting score → ending score → timeframe
forbidden: Real student names or identifiable details (PII)
Stories with fabricated score guarantees implied

fear_hooks._edge_case: Edge Case 5 — Fear hooks must NEVER cross into shame or catastrophizing
allowed: Anxiety of the unknown: 'Is your student ready?'
Time pressure: 'The SAT is 3 months away'
Missed opportunity framing: 'Most students who don't prep wish they had'
Competitive pressure: 'Other students are already preparing'
forbidden: NEVER shame the parent: 'If you don't act now, you've failed your child'
NEVER catastrophize: 'Your child's future is ruined'
NEVER use these words in fear hooks: ruined, failed, doomed, too late, worthless, behind, failure
NEVER imply the student is incapable or not smart enough
fear_hooks.required_pivot: Every fear hook MUST pivot to relief or empowerment within 1-2 sentences. The ad cannot end on fear.

allowed: Acknowledging the stress of SAT prep without judgment
Validating that one-size-fits-all prep doesn't work for everyone
Affirming the student's effort and intelligence
forbidden: Pity framing — we empower, we do not feel sorry
Implying the student has been failed by others (lawsuit risk)

rules: Match CTA to funnel stage — awareness ads use soft CTAs, conversion ads use action CTAs
Low-friction first step always outperforms high-commitment asks

image_prompt_guidelines._edge_case: Edge Case 6 — Image models cannot render typography accurately
image_prompt_guidelines.style: UGC-style (user-generated content) — authentic, real-person look. NOT polished studio photography.
allowed_subjects: Parent and teen at kitchen table, teen at laptop
Student at desk studying, looking focused or relieved
Smiling student with laptop showing score results
Parent and child having a positive conversation about school
Student in a video call with a tutor, both engaged
required_elements: Authentic, natural lighting — warm, not studio-lit
Real-looking people — not stock photo perfection
Emotion visible — relief, pride, focus, confidence
forbidden_in_image_prompts: NEVER request text, words, signs, or logos rendered inside the image
NEVER use phrases like: 'a sign that says', 'text reading', 'words that say', 'banner saying'
NEVER request brand logos or product names written in the image
NEVER request polished studio photography or commercial-looking setups

primary_text.rule: Hook must be complete within first 100 characters. Value proposition in first sentence. Minimum 80 chars to avoid stub output.

headline.rule: Benefit-driven. Often truncated on mobile — front-load the outcome.

description.rule: One sentence max. Secondary reinforcement only. Do not rely on it — often hidden on mobile.
